result:
The moon is full tonight, a silver coin pressed against a black velvet sky. It hangs above the cottonwood in my yard like an unspoken answer to a question I have forgotten to ask. I walk the dry creek bed, eyes tracing the pale braid of the Milky Way that runs from the roof of the pines to the rim of the North Ridge. Somewhere behind me a coyote splits open the quiet with three high-pitched syllables, and silence folds back together after it like water healing around a stone.

I remember how, for most of my childhood, I believed the night was empty. Empty of color, of noise, of something close enough to breath that I could lean on it whenever loneliness visited. Now I know better. The night is crowded: bats stitch frantic embroidery between the treetops, moths ghost the porch light, each carrying more memory than any of us will admit. A memory the shape of last year’s porch, the same bulb flickering like small applause for a slow surrender. I kept a box of letters beside the rocking chair then. I never mailed any of them. They aged the way fruit leathers in a cupboard, edges curling inward, sealing their secrets tighter with each passing season.

One of those letters was addressed to my father. I ended it with a map of the constellations he taught me the summer he built a telescope out of a cardboard mailing tube and the lens from a broken pair of reading glasses. I drew the Summer Triangle first in pencil, then in careful ink. Vega, Deneb, Altair—three bright freckles on the dark cheek of space. I added the label “places to look when you’re lonely” in the margin, underlined three times. I sealed the envelope, set it in the box, and blamed the postage I never bought. The truth: I feared the day might arrive when he would look through that borrowed lens and see how small we both had become.

Somewhere else in the darkness, another traveler is raising binoculars to the same moon. Maybe it’s a woman standing on the balcony of a sixth-floor walk-up in Naples, feeling the Mediterranean wind lick salt into her hair. Maybe it’s a kid in Kansas wearing cowboy pajamas, mapping mare, ocean, mountain on that luminous surface like Columbus preparing to sail. The moon reflects everybody at once, passports of light handing off from eye to mirror and back. In that sense, every gaze belongs to everyone. A thousand small explorations collapsed into a single, immense seeing.

This is what I think about when I can’t fall asleep. Wonder widens inside me like an underground lake swollen with spring thaw, and I start to inventory small mythologies. The Spanish call the full moon media luna, harvest half-lit. Hawaiians know it as mahina hoku—star-moon. In Finnish there’s kuutamo, a word that gathers the light moonlit snow gives back to dark air. Each name is a small candle left in the window of an ancient, shared house. Step close enough and you’ll feel the breath of every person who has ever waited, listening for footfalls that never came.

My father is eighty-seven now. His hands tremble too much to thread a needle or aim a telescope. When I visit, he tells me stories instead: a late-autumn bonfire built from citrus crates on the edge of the olive grove, the day the moon rose behind the flames so swollen he felt swallowed by transparent orange. He says he held out his palms as if receiving communion. I don’t know if the memory is literal or stitched from longing the way my letters were. What matters is the light it leaves on his face, slow amber spreading like warm oil. I sit beside him, match his breathing, and do not ask for proof. Some truths orbit us quietly, circling like moth shadows around a porch bulb, content never to land.

Once, when I was eleven, I tried to walk all night without artificial light. I carried no flashlight, not even matches. The grass was May cool under my bare feet. One step after another in the dark felt like dipping toes into a river whose name I couldn’t pronounce. I reached the bottom pasture where three wild sorrel horses grazed. Their luminous coats drank the moon. They regarded me, not transfixed but listening, ears flicking to fold me into their night vocabulary. I stayed until the eastern sky bruised lilac, and then—as they began to shimmer the way only horses and ghosts can—I understood the moon had been walking too. Even now, I follow its orbit through memory the same way a compass hand follows the place better than the map.

Tonight, the same plants that two weeks ago bent under snow are suddenly beaded with rescue fragrance: blooming viburnum, lemon balm struck by dew, something resinous and secret exhaling from the pinon. I inhale once, twice, as deliberately as reading Braille. The moonlight is not evenly profitable here; some shrubs glisten like pawned silverware, others remain hostage to ink. This unevenness feels honest. I have never trusted daylight’s democratic promises, the insistence that everything be illuminated and therefore equal. Night gives us the contours of desire, the edges of risk. It reminds us there are veins in which darkness thrives on brightness, the way a watercolor gains color when the paper is allowed to shine through.

I return home, wait the quiet quarter hour it takes for my eyes to adjust to dimmer rooms. On the hallway table lies a stack of blank postcards purchased my last trip to Taos: adobe churches bleeding terracotta in late sun, dancing kachinas stepping out of time. I pick one called Moonrise over San Francisco de Asís. The moon printed on the paper is cartoonishly orange yet undeniably tender. I lift my father’s fountain pen—nib dented from decades of hard consonants—and write in lowercase so the loops look like tiny lunar craters:

in the garden the lemon verbena
has learned to speak without punctuation
its green scent pauses
briefly in the comma of
your lost exhalations
then travels
until it becomes the star
we forgot to count
tonight I watched the moon the way one studies
a pocket watch inherited
from a grandfather who believed every second
could be polished
into a smaller one

I sign simply, your daughter. The postcard will ride the mailstream tomorrow, a thin slip of light carrying more night inside it than anyone will guess. On the back a barcode will appear, machine-printed, but between the bars I spot the constellation of Orion: stars glimmering like breadcrumb promises spilled on asphalt.

I slip the card into the mailbox, raise the flag. The hinge squeaks with surrender. For a moment balance trembles between the hand letting go and the possibility of retrieving the letter. Then gravity wins, and the metal door cools against my knuckles. Whispering thanks to the mail, to the moon, to horses grazing decades ago, I walk back into the house. Before I shut the door, I turn and look one final time. The night still glows, but a new note underlines it—a hush with anticipation in its mouth, same hush felt before a curtain rises. Something is about to begin. It always is.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
